era_context: The early-to-mid 14th century: the Hundred Years' War is beginning (1337); the Black Death is spreading along trade routes; the Yuan Dynasty is fragmenting; the Mamluk Sultanate is at its height; and European feudalism is under structural stress.

events.title: Clement VI elected Pope
events.description: Pierre Roger was elected Pope Clement VI on 7 May 1342. He succeeded Benedict XII and would serve until 1352. Clement VI was known for his patronage of the arts, his diplomatic skill, and his later response to the Black Death. His election marked a continuation of the Avignon Papacy (1309–1377), during which the papal court was based in Avignon rather than Rome. Clement VI would play a significant role in European politics during the Hundred Years' War and the early stages of the plague.
events.certainty_note: Papal succession dates are precisely documented in contemporary records.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: Early campaigns and skirmishes
events.description: In 1342, the Hundred Years' War (1337–1453) was in its early phase. Edward III of England had claimed the French throne in 1337, and military campaigns were ongoing. In 1342, there were skirmishes in Aquitaine and Brittany, but no major pitched battles. The conflict was driven by dynastic claims, territorial disputes, and economic competition. The war would define European politics for over a century.
events.certainty_note: The broader conflict is well-documented; specific 1342 events are less detailed.

events.title: Black Death spreads in Asia; plague in Central Asia
events.description: The bubonic plague (Yersinia pestis) was spreading along Silk Road trade routes in 1342. Chinese sources document plague deaths in Central Asia and possibly in China during this period. The exact chronology is debated, but by 1342, the pandemic was well-established in Asia. The disease would reach the Mediterranean by 1347, devastating Europe.

sources.name: Yuan Shi and Chinese local records

events.certainty_note: Plague's presence in Asia by 1342 is supported by Chinese sources, though exact dating varies.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Emperor Shun; internal instability
events.description: In 1342, the Yuan Dynasty under Emperor Shun (Toghon Temür, r. 1333–1368) was experiencing severe internal conflict. Factional struggles among Mongol nobility, Han Chinese officials, and regional warlords were destabilizing the empire. Famine and peasant unrest were growing. The empire would ultimately collapse to the Ming Dynasty (1368). In 1342, the Yuan was still nominally powerful but increasingly fragile.
key_figures: Toghon Temür (Emperor Shun of Yuan, r. 1333–1368)

sources.name: Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan, compiled by Ming historians)

events.certainty_note: Yuan instability in the 1340s is well-documented; specific 1342 events are less detailed.

events.title: Mali Empire under Mansa Maghan I; West African trade
events.description: In 1342, Mali was ruled by Mansa Maghan I (r. c. 1337–1341 or c. 1341–1360, dates disputed). Mali was a major power in West African gold trade and Islamic scholarship. The empire controlled trans-Saharan trade routes and maintained diplomatic relations with North African and Middle Eastern powers. In 1342, Mali's influence was still strong, though the empire was beginning to face competition from rising regional powers.
key_figures: Mansa Maghan I (r. c. 1337–1341 or c. 1341–1360, dates disputed)

sources.name: Tarikh al-Sudan and Tarikh al-Fattash (West African chronicles)

events.certainty_note: Mali's political history in the 1340s relies on oral tradition and later chronicles. Succession dates are disputed.

disconfirming_evidence: Clement VI's election date (7 May 1342) is sometimes given as 1343 in older sources. Mali's rulers in the 1340s are often conflated or misnamed; the exact succession and dates are disputed among historians. The plague's spread in 1342 is less well-documented than in 1344–1347; some scholars argue the pandemic's major impact in Asia occurred slightly later.
historiographic_note: 1342 is a year of gradual change rather than dramatic events. European sources focus on the Hundred Years' War and papal politics. Chinese sources document Yuan instability. West African history relies on oral tradition and later chronicles, with significant gaps. The plague's spread is documented retrospectively. Bias toward European and East Asian history is evident.

graph_edges.note: Clement VI's election in 1342 positioned him to respond to the plague in 1347
Early Hundred Years' War campaigns in 1342 led to the Battle of Crécy in 1346
Plague spread in Asia in 1342 reached the Mediterranean in 1347
